verdict_reasoning: CRISP1 (Cysteine-Rich Secretory Protein 1) is a secreted protein of the CRISP family predominantly expressed in the epididymis and deposited onto sperm during epididymal transit. It lacks a transmembrane domain and is not constitutively displayed on the outer leaflet of somatic cell plasma membranes. However, CRISP1 binds tightly to the sperm surface — both to the dorsal head (loose, removable fraction) and to the equatorial segment (tightly bound, wash-resistant fraction that persists through the acrosome reaction). The equatorial-segment pool has been demonstrated to remain stably surface-associated on mature sperm even after extensive washing, consistent with a wash-resistant non-covalent or covalent tethering to a sperm-surface TM partner. This makes it contextual under tissue_restricted_surface (gamete/germline-restricted) and potentially stable_surface_attachment. The surface story is restricted to a narrow germline/reproductive lineage (epididymal sperm). No documented broad somatic surface pool, no TM domain of its own.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Precise molecular basis of the wash-resistant sperm-surface association (covalent vs. very tight non-covalent) and whether it truly qualifies as stable_surface_attachment vs. tissue_restricted_surface in the contextual taxonomy.
